summary: Personal calorie tracker app where users can upload food photos to get AI-based calorie estimates (via Open Food Facts API, free, no API key required), log daily food intake, track step count and calories burned, and view a full daily record of calories in vs. calories out. Features include: delete food entries with immediate calorie recalculation, a 7-day calorie consumption progress chart with calorie limit reference line, a persistent per-day calorie limit setting with calories-remaining display, and an in-app visual warning when the calorie limit is exceeded. Aimed at helping users reduce body fat.
architectural_notes: Food calorie estimation uses the Open Food Facts REST API (free, no API key required) queried by food name derived from the uploaded image filename/description.
known_issues: Deployment error encountered; reviewing full codebase for Motoko/React issues (type mismatches, missing exports, import errors, build configuration problems)
Daily Dashboard date selector shows incorrect date instead of defaulting to today's actual date

existing_features.title: Build a Food Upload screen where the user can upload or select a photo of food. The app must read the image, display a preview, and perform a client-side heuristic analysis of the image filename and basic color/size signals to produce a plausible calorie estimate range. Present the estimated calories and a suggested food name to the user, who can confirm or manually adjust both values before saving the entry to the backend for the current date.

Build a Daily Dashboard screen (defaulting to today's date) showing: a date selector to switch days, a list of all food entries logged for that day (food name, calories, thumbnail), total calories consumed, a step count input where the user can log their steps for the day, total calories burned from steps, and a net calorie balance indicator (calories in minus calories out). Display a motivational message indicating whether the user is in a calorie deficit or surplus.

Build a History Log screen that lists all dates that have logged food entries or step data. For each date, show the total calories consumed, total calories burned, and net calories. Tapping or clicking a date navigates to that date's Daily Dashboard. Display a summary trend (e.g., average daily net calories across all logged days).

Design and apply a cohesive visual theme across the entire app. Use a fresh, health-and-wellness-inspired aesthetic: clean white and soft green tones with warm orange accents, rounded cards, clear typography with strong hierarchy, and an airy layout. The design should feel motivating and approachable for a daily fat-loss tracking use case.

Fix the step count input on the Daily Dashboard so that when the user enters a step count and confirms, the value is correctly persisted to the backend via the logSteps (or equivalent) mutation. After a successful save, the displayed calories burned and net calorie balance must immediately reflect the updated step count without requiring a page reload.

On the Daily Dashboard, add a delete button (trash icon) to each food entry card. When clicked, call the deleteFoodEntry backend mutation, then immediately invalidate and refresh the daily summary so that total calories consumed and net calorie balance update without a page reload.

On the Daily Dashboard, add a calorie limit input control that loads the current day's persisted limit from the backend via getCalorieLimit, allows the user to set or update it (calling setCalorieLimit on save), and displays a 'calories remaining' counter (limit minus calories consumed). When calories consumed exceed the limit, show a prominent in-app visual warning banner or indicator on the dashboard.

Add a 7-day calorie consumption progress chart to the Daily Dashboard (or a dedicated chart section) that plots daily total calories consumed for the past 7 days as a bar or line chart, with the current day's calorie limit rendered as a horizontal reference line.

On the Food Upload screen, after the user selects or drops a food image, extract the food name from the image filename (strip extension, replace hyphens/underscores with spaces, title-case) and query the Open Food Facts REST API (https://world.openfoodfacts.org/cgi/search.pl) with that term to retrieve real nutritional data. Parse the response to extract the calorie value (energy-kcal_100g or nutriments.energy-kcal) from the best-matching product. Use this API-derived calorie value and product name to pre-populate the food name and calorie estimate fields, replacing the existing client-side heuristic estimate. If the API returns no usable result or the request fails, fall back silently to the existing heuristic estimate from calorieEstimator.ts. Display a loading indicator while the API request is in flight. The user must still be able to review and manually adjust both the food name and calorie value before confirming and saving the entry to the backend.

Audit all frontend TypeScript/React files for build errors including: missing or incorrect imports, type errors in actor call return types (especially Motoko variant/option types being destructured incorrectly), missing module exports, and misconfigured build or Tailwind paths. Fix all errors so the frontend builds and serves successfully.

Verify that the dfx.json, package.json, and any Vite/build configuration files are correctly set up so that the frontend can reach the deployed backend canister (correct canister IDs, correct environment variable references, correct network configuration). Fix any misconfiguration that would cause the frontend to fail to connect to the backend at runtime.

Fix the Daily Dashboard date initialization so that when the page loads, the selected date state is set to today's actual current date (using `new Date()` or equivalent), and the date selector/calendar input correctly reflects and displays today's true date. Ensure that navigating to the dashboard without a pre-selected date always defaults to the current calendar date, not a hardcoded or stale value.

new_feature_requests.summary: Food photo upload with AI calorie estimation
Daily food intake logging with calorie totals
Step count tracking with calories burned calculation
Daily dashboard showing calories in vs. calories out (net calories)
History log of daily calorie intake and burn records

Create a backend data model and actor to store user food log entries per day. Each entry must include: date (Text), food name (Text), calorie estimate (Nat), and an image reference (Text). Provide functions to add a food entry, retrieve all entries for a given date, and retrieve a list of all distinct logged dates.

Create a backend data model and functions to store daily step count records. Each record includes: date (Text) and step count (Nat). Provide functions to set/update steps for a date and retrieve steps for a given date. Calculate calories burned from steps using the standard approximation of 0.04 kcal per step.

Create a backend function to return a full daily summary for a given date, including: total calories consumed (sum of all food entries), total steps taken, total calories burned from steps, and net calories (consumed minus burned).
Add delete option for each food entry in the daily log; backend deleteFoodEntry function; immediately update total calories consumed and net calorie balance after deletion
Daily progress chart showing calorie consumption for the past 7 days with the calorie limit plotted as a reference line
Persistent per-day calorie limit stored in the backend, with a calories-remaining display on the dashboard and an in-app visual warning banner when the limit is exceeded
Add a deleteFoodEntry(id: Text, date: Text) update function to the backend actor that removes a food entry by its ID for the given date and returns a success/failure result.
Add a setCalorieLimit(date: Text, limit: Nat) update function and a getCalorieLimit(date: Text) query function to the backend actor so the daily calorie limit is persisted per date across sessions.

Integrate Open Food Facts API (free, no API key) into the food upload screen to replace the heuristic calorie estimate with real database-backed nutritional data based on the detected food name from the uploaded image; user can still review and adjust before confirming.

Audit the Motoko backend (backend/main.mo and backend/migration.mo) for compilation errors including: type mismatches, undefined identifiers, incorrect function signatures, missing or incorrect imports, and any incompatible use of List/Map/Blob APIs. Fix all errors so the backend compiles and deploys successfully.
Fix the Daily Dashboard date selector so it correctly defaults to today's actual date on load and the calendar/date picker reflects the true current date.
